So running experiments, one thing I find very helpful
is workflow automation.
And basically, what I like to do is I
like to modularize each step of an experiment
into a directory where you have a directory,
a simple and a directory is output.
This is my personal way of doing things.
There are other ways of doing things that are also good.
But very often, just to give an example,
you'll need to do pre-processing according to some.
You'll need to do data selection.
So you'll need to select which data sets you're training on.
You'll need to do pre-processing of them
with the tokenization model.
And then you will need to run an experiment.
And then you'll need to do evaluation.
And those are all kind of like discrete steps.
Where the data selection takes in your big pool of data
and outputs a data set that's been selected.
The tokenization will take a tokenizer model.
Maybe train the tokenizer model and split it up
into different tokens.
The training will train.
It might output a whole bunch of checkpoints.
And the evaluation will evaluate one checkpoint.
And so those are all kind of modular.
And you can actually think of each one of them
as like a function in your pipeline program.
And you kind of want to avoid rerunning data set selection
and tokenization every time you do a new evaluation.
Like that would be kind of silly.
You definitely want to avoid rerunning training every time
you evaluate a checkpoint.
So what I do is I often name directories by parameters
where it's like transformer layer 8, node 512,
drop out 0.5, label smooth 0.02.

How am I going to support them by the experiments
that I'm going to show in the result section?
And this identifies unjustified experimental claims.
So let's say your method is you're
saying something like this method improves
